---
title: "National Research Platform (Nautilus)"
author:
  - name: Chris Endemann
    email: endemann@wisc.edu
date: 2026-05-13
date-modified: 2026-05-13
date-format: long
image: "../../../images/vincent-van-zalinge-mDohQISBnCk-unsplash.jpg"
categories:
  - Compute
  - GPU
  - HPC
  - LLM
  - Kubernetes
  - Jupyter
  - NSF
---

The [National Research Platform (NRP)](https://nrp.ai/) — sometimes called **Nautilus** — is an NSF-supported, distributed Kubernetes cluster that stitches together GPU servers, storage, and high-speed networking across dozens of sites. It is **free** for researchers at [InCommon](https://incommon.org/)-affiliated institutions (including UW–Madison) who are doing sponsored AI/ML research, subject to NRP's [Acceptable Use Policy](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/start/policies/) and fair-use limits.

NRP is most useful in two ways for ML/AI researchers:

1. **Compute** — Kubernetes-orchestrated GPU jobs (RTX 2080 Ti, 3090, 4090, A10, A100) and a JupyterHub for interactive notebook work.
2. **Managed LLM inference** — A free, OpenAI-compatible API endpoint serving open-weight models (Qwen3, GPT-OSS, Kimi, GLM-5, Gemma, MiniMax-M2, OLMo, embeddings).

::: {.callout-tip}
## Get started
Join the [Nautilus Matrix channel](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/start/contact/) first — a Matrix account is required before you can request a Nautilus account. From there, follow [Get Access](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/start/get-access/) to register and be promoted to User.
:::

## Why use NRP?

- **Free GPU and LLM access** for AI/ML research at participating institutions — no cloud bills, no grant overhead.
- **Distributed across dozens of sites** — jobs land wherever capacity is available, and you don't manage the placement.
- **Hosted LLM API** with current open-weight models, callable from any OpenAI-compatible client (Python `openai` SDK, LangChain, LlamaIndex, etc.).
- **JupyterLab option** — if you don't want to learn Kubernetes, request access to NRP's hosted JupyterHub and write code in the browser.
- **Non-commercial / non-profit** — usage is governed by the NRP [AUP](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/start/policies/); research and educational use only.

## Managed LLM inference

NRP runs a [managed LLM gateway](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/ai/llm-managed/overview/) backed by GPU nodes contributed by the San Diego Supercomputer Center (SDSC) and Internet2. It exposes an OpenAI-compatible API and a web chat UI, with a rotating roster of open-weight models.

### Available models (as of May 2026)

| Family | Examples | Notes |
|--------|----------|-------|
| General chat | `qwen3`, `qwen3-small`, `gpt-oss`, `kimi`, `glm-5`, `minimax-m2` | Frontier-quality open-weight models |
| Smaller / fast | `gemma`, `gemma-small`, `olmo` | Lower latency, lower context |
| Embeddings | `qwen3-embedding` | For RAG / vector search |

See the [Available models](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/ai/llm-managed/models/) page for the current list, context lengths, and chat interfaces. Models come and go — check the [lifecycle & changelog](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/ai/llm-managed/changelog/) before depending on a specific name in production scripts.

### Fair-use limits

Limits are **per-user concurrency** (not requests-per-minute). They are not yet auto-enforced, but rate limiting in the gateway is "coming soon," and admins ask users to self-regulate.

| Max concurrent requests | Models |
|-------------------------|--------|
| 2 | `kimi` |
| 4 | `glm-5` |
| 8 | `minimax-m2`, `qwen3-small`, `gemma`, `gemma-small`, `olmo` |
| 16 | `qwen3`, `gpt-oss`, `qwen3-embedding` |

A few important caveats from the [fair-use policy](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/ai/llm-managed/fair-use/):

- **Long-context requests count more.** Any request using ≥35% of a model's context length is limited to **1 concurrent request per user**, and the *sum* of in-flight context across your requests must stay under 35%.
- **SDSC and Internet2 affiliates get 2× limits**, plus the option to arrange higher-volume sessions.
- **Need more than the limits?** Contact admins in advance (e.g., for a deadline or a workshop) — or [deploy your own model](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/ai/managing/) on the cluster.
- **Scripts should retry indefinitely** with exponential backoff and dynamically reduce concurrency when latency climbs. Models go down for maintenance.

### Calling the API

NRP's LLM endpoint is OpenAI-compatible, so any client that accepts a custom `base_url` works. See [API access](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/ai/llm-managed/api/) and [client configurations](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/ai/llm-managed/clients/) for token setup and example clients (Python `openai`, `litellm`, LangChain, OpenWebUI, Continue, etc.).

::: {.callout-note}
NRP's managed LLMs are great for prototyping, evaluation, and research that benefits from open-weight reproducibility. For proprietary frontier models (Claude, GPT-4o, Gemini) and HIPAA-eligible inference, see [GenAI at UW-Madison](../GenAI/GenAI-at-UW-Madison.qmd) and [UW Cloud Services](UW-Cloud-Services.qmd).
:::

## Compute (Kubernetes pods and Jupyter)

NRP is a Kubernetes cluster — you describe workloads in YAML and submit them with `kubectl`. The [Using Nautilus](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/running/intro/) guides walk through pods, scaling, batch jobs, scheduling, container images, and persistent storage.

GPU hardware spans both general-purpose (RTX 2080 Ti, 3090, 4090, A10) and high-precision (A100) cards, distributed across many sites. Specialized hardware (e.g., Qualcomm Cloud AI 100 cards) is also available — see the [docs](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/ai/cloud-ai-100/).

If you don't want to learn Kubernetes, NRP also offers a hosted [JupyterHub](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/ai/jupyterhub/) for interactive notebook work — browser-based, no local installs, backed by cluster GPUs.

## When to choose NRP vs. other compute

NRP overlaps with several resources UW–Madison researchers already use. Quick guidance:

| Use case | Recommendation |
|----------|----------------|
| Free, hosted LLM API for prototyping or research | **NRP managed LLMs** — open-weight models, no cost |
| Batch ML training, hyperparameter sweeps (UW–Madison) | [**CHTC**](CHTC.qmd) — free GPU Lab, dedicated facilitators |
| Quick interactive notebook, no setup (UW–Madison) | [**BadgerCompute**](BadgerCompute.qmd) or [**Google Colab**](GoogleColab.qmd) |
| NVLink multi-GPU, persistent endpoints, managed ML platforms | [**UW Cloud Services**](UW-Cloud-Services.qmd) (AWS / GCP / Azure) |
| Multi-site GPU jobs, open-weight LLM API, Kubernetes-native workflows | **NRP / Nautilus** |
| Frontier proprietary LLMs (Claude, GPT-4o, Gemini) | [**GenAI at UW-Madison**](../GenAI/GenAI-at-UW-Madison.qmd) |

The honest tradeoff: NRP gives you free access to compute and to a serious roster of open-weight LLMs, but it expects you to learn Kubernetes (or use the JupyterHub) and to play nicely under shared fair-use limits. CHTC is friendlier for batch ML at UW and has dedicated facilitators; cloud services are friendlier for managed platforms and NVLink. NRP shines when you want a free open-weight LLM API or when CHTC's queue is blocking you and you're willing to write some YAML.

## Connections to the ML Marathon

The [ML+X Machine Learning Marathon](../../Projects/ML-Marathon/index.qmd) is a 12-week ML/AI hackathon where teams tackle real-world challenges. NRP is a natural fit:

- **Free LLM API for GenAI / RAG challenges.** Teams building retrieval-augmented pipelines, agentic workflows, or LLM-based evaluators can call `qwen3`, `gpt-oss`, or `kimi` from any OpenAI-compatible client — no credit card needed, no per-token billing to plan around. `qwen3-embedding` covers the vector-search side.
- **Free GPUs for fine-tuning and inference on open weights.** Teams that want to fine-tune Llama, Qwen, Gemma, or similar can request GPU pods on NRP without burning grant funds or competing in CHTC's queue.
- **Reproducibility.** Open-weight models hosted by a non-profit research platform are easier to cite and revisit after the Marathon than closed APIs whose pricing or model versions may change.
- **Plan for fair-use limits.** With per-user concurrency capped (often 8–16, lower for the largest models), teams should design pipelines that batch sequentially, retry with backoff, and avoid >35% context usage per request. This is good engineering hygiene regardless of platform.
- **Mix platforms.** Many teams will combine NRP (open-weight LLM API), CHTC (training and batch sweeps), and UW Cloud (occasional managed-service needs). None of these are mutually exclusive.

If you're organizing a Marathon challenge that needs NRP capacity beyond fair-use limits, contact the NRP admins in advance via the Matrix channel to arrange a higher-volume session.

## Policies and acceptable use

- NRP is **non-profit and non-commercial**. All usage is subject to the [AUP](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/start/policies/).
- Do not use NRP for restricted/regulated data unless you have explicit confirmation from admins that your data class is permitted.
- Standard data hygiene applies: don't store secrets in container images, don't share credentials, follow your institution's data-handling rules.

## Getting help

- **Matrix channel** (required for account creation): see [Asking for Support](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/start/contact/)
- **Documentation**: [nrp.ai/documentation](https://nrp.ai/documentation/)
- **FAQ**: [nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/start/faq/](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/start/faq/)
- **Glossary**: [nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/start/glossary/](https://nrp.ai/documentation/userdocs/start/glossary/)
- **AI/ML chat room** (for LLM-specific questions, latency reports, and capacity requests): linked from the Nautilus Matrix space

## Related resources

- [**Compute**: Center for High Throughput Computing (CHTC)](CHTC.qmd) — Free GPU Lab and HTC pool for UW–Madison researchers; better for batch ML training and large hyperparameter sweeps.
- [**Compute**: UW–Madison Cloud Services (AWS, GCP, Azure)](UW-Cloud-Services.qmd) — When you need NVLink multi-GPU, managed ML platforms, or persistent services.
- [**Compute**: BadgerCompute](BadgerCompute.qmd) — UW–Madison's lightweight, NetID-authenticated JupyterHub for quick interactive work.
- [**Compute**: Google Colab](GoogleColab.qmd) — Free hosted notebooks with optional GPU access for prototyping.
- [**GenAI**: UW Generative AI Services & Policies](../GenAI/GenAI-at-UW-Madison.qmd) — UW-vetted access to proprietary frontier models (Claude, GPT-4o, Gemini) and policy guidance.
- [**Projects**: ML+X Machine Learning Marathon](../../Projects/ML-Marathon/index.qmd) — Annual ML/AI hackathon; NRP's free LLM API and GPUs are well suited to Marathon team workflows.

## Comments
